title: How to Configure Microsoft Purview Sensitivity Labels and Label Policies

content:
Identity-based attacks rose 32% in H1 2025, with AiTM phishing and impersonation campaigns driving most of that growth (Microsoft Digital Defense Report, 2025). Microsoft Defender for Office 365 anti-phishing policies are the primary defence against these attacks, specifically stopping the email delivery that makes impersonation and AiTM campaigns possible. If your policies aren’t explicitly configured, you’re relying on defaults that were never designed to stop modern phishing kits.
Phishing awareness and AiTM bypass tactics make anti-phishing configuration in Defender for Office 365 critical. Photo: Markus Winkler / Pexels.
Key Takeaways
Anti-phishing impersonation protection requires MDO Plan 1 or higher; it is not included in EOP (Exchange Online Protection) alone Spoof intelligence is on by default; impersonation protection for specific users and domains must be explicitly enabled AiTM phishing bypasses MFA by stealing the session cookie after authentication succeeds, not by stealing the password Standard and Strict preset security policies are the recommended starting point; custom policies are for exceptions E3 includes EOP only; E5 or the MDO add-on is required for impersonation protection features (Microsoft, 2025) Why Does AiTM Phishing Bypass MFA? Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) phishing doesn’t steal passwords. It sits as a reverse proxy between the user and a legitimate authentication service, relaying the authentication in real time. The user completes MFA successfully, the attacker captures the resulting session cookie, and then replays that cookie from a different device. MFA was never asked for again. The attack didn’t break MFA; it moved past it entirely.
The Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2025 names AiTM and device code phishing as the two primary techniques behind the 32% rise in identity attacks in H1 2025. Both techniques specifically target the post-authentication token, not the credential itself. Standard anti-phishing policies that focus on link rewriting and malware scanning don’t stop these attacks at the delivery stage. Only policies targeting the phishing infrastructure do.
This is exactly why MDO anti-phishing configuration matters. Blocking the phishing email before the user clicks is the only reliable control point you have. Once the session cookie is captured, detection and response become your only options. Prevention is cheaper.
MFA still blocks over 99% of automated credential attacks (Microsoft Digital Defense Report, 2025). AiTM attacks are sophisticated, targeted campaigns, not mass spray-and-pray. But 60% of breaches still involve a human element (Verizon DBIR, 2025), and credential abuse accounts for 22% of initial access vectors in confirmed breaches (Verizon DBIR, 2025). The combination means targeted phishing remains high-value.
What License Do You Need for Anti-Phishing Protection? Impersonation protection is not included in Exchange Online Protection, which is what E3 provides by default. This surprises many administrators who assume all anti-phishing features come with the base Microsoft 365 subscription. The table below shows exactly what you get at each tier.
Capability EOP (included with E3) MDO Plan 1 MDO Plan 2 Anti-spoofing / spoof intelligence Yes Yes Yes Anti-phishing (basic) Yes Yes Yes User impersonation protection No Yes Yes Domain impersonation protection No Yes Yes Mailbox intelligence impersonation No Yes Yes Safe Links / Safe Attachments No Yes Yes Threat Explorer No No Yes Attack Simulation Training No No Yes Advanced Hunting No No Yes MDO Plan 1 is included in Microsoft 365 Business Premium and available as a standalone add-on for E3 users. MDO Plan 2 is included in Microsoft 365 E5 and Microsoft 365 E5 Security. If you’re on E3 without the MDO add-on, you have spoof intelligence and basic anti-phishing, but impersonation protection for specific users and domains is not available (Microsoft Service Descriptions, 2025).
Should You Start with Preset Security Policies? Yes. Microsoft’s preset security policies, Standard and Strict, are the right starting point for nearly every organisation. They include anti-phishing protection at tested thresholds and are updated automatically by Microsoft as the threat landscape changes. Building a custom policy from scratch means taking on the maintenance burden of keeping thresholds current.
Standard protection is the baseline for all users. Strict protection adds higher aggressiveness and is designed for priority accounts: executives, finance staff, IT administrators, and anyone whose inbox is a high-value target. The recommended approach is to apply the Standard preset to all users first, then layer the Strict preset on top of priority account groups.
Policy priority order determines which policy wins when multiple rules match the same recipient. Strict takes precedence over Standard, custom policies apply by number (lower number = higher priority), and the default policy applies last (Microsoft Learn, 2025).
Configure preset policies at: https://security.microsoft.com/presetSecurityPolicies
How Do You Configure Anti-Phishing Impersonation Protection? Impersonation protection requires explicit configuration. No users or domains are protected by default, even with MDO in place. You must add the specific accounts and domains you want protected, then set the actions to take when impersonation is detected.
Navigate to: https://security.microsoft.com/antiphishing
Create a new policy or edit an existing custom policy. Work through the impersonation settings in this order.
User Impersonation Protection Add up to 350 specific users whose identities you want to protect. These are typically executives, finance staff, IT admins, and legal. The policy monitors inbound email for senders who appear to be impersonating these users based on display name similarity and email domain lookalikes.
Set the action to Quarantine the message for impersonated users. Moving to junk is too lenient for high-value account impersonation.
Domain Impersonation Protection Add your own accepted domains and any partner domains you regularly receive email from. MDO will flag inbound messages from domains that closely resemble your protected domains: typosquats, homoglyph substitutions, and lookalike domains.
You can add up to 1,024 entries across the trusted senders and domains list. Include your primary domain and any secondary or legacy domains your organisation uses.
Mailbox Intelligence and Intelligence-Based Impersonation Protection Mailbox intelligence is on by default in all anti-phishing policies. It learns normal communication patterns for each mailbox. The intelligence-based impersonation protection toggle is separate and must be enabled explicitly. It uses that learned communication graph to identify messages from senders the user has never corresponded with but whose display names match protected users (Microsoft Learn, 2025).
First-Contact Safety Tips Enable first-contact safety tips. When a recipient receives an email from a sender for the first time, a yellow banner appears in the message notifying them. This is a low-friction user awareness control that doesn’t require any training programme.
The first-contact safety tip is one of the most underused controls in MDO. It’s entirely passive from the user’s perspective but measurably increases the time users spend evaluating unfamiliar senders. Combined with user impersonation protection, it creates a second signal path: the policy acts on the back end, the safety tip acts on the front end.
How Does Spoof Intelligence Work? Spoof intelligence is on by default in every anti-phishing policy, including those in the base EOP tier available with E3. It monitors all domains that send email on behalf of your organisation and automatically classifies senders as allowed or blocked based on email authentication results (SPF, DKIM, DMARC).
Two visual indicators appear in Outlook and Outlook on the Web when spoof intelligence detects issues. A question mark on the sender photo indicates the message failed SPF or DKIM authentication and also failed DMARC. A “via” tag appears when the DKIM signing domain or the MAIL FROM domain differs from the From address. Both indicators are visible to end users without any configuration.
Manage allow and block entries for spoofed senders at: https://security.microsoft.com/spoofintelligence
Override entries in the Tenant Allow/Block List under the Spoofed senders tab never expire. Review this list periodically to remove stale overrides, particularly for vendors or partners whose email infrastructure has changed.
One important note: do not disable anti-spoofing if your MX record doesn’t point directly to Microsoft 365. If you route inbound mail through a third-party gateway or on-premises server before it reaches Exchange Online, the inbound IP will always fail SPF checks and anti-spoofing will block legitimate mail. Use Enhanced Filtering for Connectors instead. This configuration tells MDO to evaluate the original sending IP, not the gateway IP.
Spoof intelligence in Microsoft Defender for Office 365 automatically evaluates all inbound messages against SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records, classifying senders as allowed or blocked without administrator intervention. It is included in Exchange Online Protection at no additional cost and is active by default in all anti-phishing policies. Override entries for spoofed senders are managed in the Tenant Allow/Block List and do not expire automatically. (Microsoft Learn, 2025)
PowerShell: Automate Anti-Phishing Policy Configuration The Microsoft 365 Defender portal gives you full GUI coverage for anti-phishing policies. PowerShell is the right choice when you need to deploy consistent policy configurations across multiple tenants or want to bring policy state under version control.
The script below uses the ExchangeOnlineManagement v3 module with parameter splatting for readability. It creates a named anti-phishing policy with all impersonation and spoof intelligence controls enabled, then creates a rule that applies the policy to a specific recipient domain.
#Requires -Modules ExchangeOnlineManagement Connect-ExchangeOnline -ShowBanner:$false $policyParams = @{ Name = 'Impersonation Protection Policy' EnableMailboxIntelligence = $true EnableMailboxIntelligenceProtection = $true EnableOrganizationDomainsProtection = $true EnableSimilarUsersSafetyTips = $true EnableSimilarDomainsSafetyTips = $true EnableUnusualCharactersSafetyTips = $true MailboxIntelligenceProtectionAction = 'Quarantine' EnableSpoofIntelligence = $true AuthenticationFailAction = 'MoveToJmf' EnableFirstContactSafetyTips = $true ErrorAction = 'Stop' } New-AntiPhishPolicy @policyParams $ruleParams = @{ Name = 'Impersonation Protection Rule' AntiPhishPolicy = 'Impersonation Protection Policy' RecipientDomainIs = 'contoso.com' Priority = 0 ErrorAction = 'Stop' } New-AntiPhishRule @ruleParams The EnableOrganizationDomainsProtection parameter enables domain impersonation protection for all accepted domains in the tenant automatically. You don’t need to enumerate them individually.
To protect specific users from impersonation, add the TargetedUsersToProtect parameter to New-AntiPhishPolicy. The parameter accepts an array of strings in the format "Display Name;email@domain.com". Add one entry per protected user, up to 350 total.
To audit your existing policy configuration, use Get-AntiPhishPolicy | Select-Object Name, EnableMailboxIntelligenceProtection, EnableOrganizationDomainsProtection, EnableSpoofIntelligence to verify which controls are active across all policies in the tenant.
How Do You Monitor Anti-Phishing Detections? Monitoring is split across two views depending on your MDO licence tier. Both are available from the Microsoft Defender portal.
For all MDO customers, the anti-phishing insights page at https://security.microsoft.com/antiphishing provides a summary of detections by policy and by detection type. The Insights tab shows trends over the selected time period.
The spoof intelligence insight at https://security.microsoft.com/spoofintelligence shows the full list of domains that have been auto-classified, along with the infrastructure each sender uses. Review this weekly when first deploying to catch legitimate senders that have been incorrectly blocked.
MDO Plan 2 users can access Threat Explorer at https://security.microsoft.com/threatexplorer. The Phish view in Threat Explorer shows full campaign correlation data, including which users were targeted by the same phishing kit, the sending infrastructure, and the URLs used. This is where investigation starts when a user reports a suspicious message.
MDO Plan 1 users have Real-time Detections, which provides the same Phish view without campaign correlation. You can pivot from a single message to see other messages from the same sender, but the automated campaign grouping is an MDO Plan 2 feature (Microsoft Learn, 2025).
In practice, the spoof intelligence insight page generates the most actionable findings in the first two weeks after deployment. Third-party senders that use shared sending infrastructure on behalf of your domain are frequently classified as blocked. Check the page daily for the first fortnight and build up your allow list before end users notice delivery failures.
Frequently Asked Questions What is the difference between anti-phishing in EOP and Defender for Office 365? EOP anti-phishing covers spoof intelligence and basic header-based detection. MDO adds user impersonation protection, domain impersonation protection, and mailbox intelligence-based detection. These additional controls require MDO Plan 1 or higher, which is included in M365 Business Premium and M365 E5, or available as an add-on for E3. (Microsoft Service Descriptions, 2025)
Does anti-phishing protection stop AiTM phishing? MDO anti-phishing targets the delivery mechanism, blocking the phishing email before the user can click. It doesn’t operate after authentication. AiTM attacks that successfully deliver their lure to the inbox and receive a click still succeed post-delivery. Layering Conditional Access sign-in frequency policies and token protection alongside MDO anti-phishing is the more complete control combination.
What does spoof intelligence do in Microsoft 365? Spoof intelligence monitors all inbound messages and evaluates whether the sending domain passes SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. It automatically classifies senders as allowed or blocked and surfaces the results in the Tenant Allow/Block List. It is included in EOP at no additional cost and is on by default in all anti-phishing policies.
How many users can I protect with user impersonation protection? Each anti-phishing policy supports up to 350 protected users. You can create multiple custom policies with different user sets and apply them to different recipient groups to work around this limit. In practice, 350 covers most organisations’ executive, finance, and IT admin populations without needing multiple policies.
Do I need MDO Plan 2 for anti-phishing policies? No. Anti-phishing policies with full impersonation protection are available in MDO Plan 1. MDO Plan 2 adds Threat Explorer for advanced campaign investigation, Attack Simulation Training, and Advanced Hunting. For policy configuration and basic monitoring, Plan 1 is sufficient. E3 includes EOP but not MDO; E5 includes MDO Plan 2.
summary: Identity-based attacks rose 32% in H1 2025, with AiTM phishing and impersonation campaigns driving most of that growth (Microsoft Digital Defense Report, 2025). Microsoft Defender for Office 365 anti-phishing policies are the primary defence against these attacks, specifically stopping the email delivery that makes impersonation and AiTM campaigns possible. If your policies aren’t explicitly configured, you’re relying on defaults that were never designed to stop modern phishing kits.
title: Defender for Office 365 Anti-Phishing: Stop Impersonation and AiTM Attacks

content:
The IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024 puts the average breach cost at $4.88 million — and breaches traced to malicious insiders average nearly double that. DLP policies applied to email and SharePoint miss the most common exfiltration path: a user copying a file to a USB drive, printing it to a personal printer, or uploading it via an unmanaged browser. Microsoft Purview Endpoint DLP closes that gap. This post walks you through onboarding Windows devices, configuring policies with sensitive information type conditions, setting enforcement actions, and monitoring activity in Activity Explorer.
If you’re new to DLP policy anatomy — rules, conditions, and actions — read the Microsoft 365 DLP policy configuration guide first. That post covers Exchange, SharePoint, and Teams. This post covers the endpoint workload only.
Endpoint DLP enforces policy at the device, not the perimeter. Photo: Ales Nesetril / Unsplash.
Key Takeaways
Endpoint DLP requires Microsoft 365 E5 or the Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance add-on — E3 does not include it. Policies apply to 13 monitored activities including USB copy, print, cloud upload, Bluetooth, clipboard, and Windows Recall snapshots. Three enforcement modes — Audit, Block with override, and Block — let you roll out safely without locking down users immediately. Activity Explorer gives you a searchable log of every policy match at the device level, filterable by activity type, user, and location. How Endpoint DLP Differs from Exchange and SharePoint DLP Standard DLP policies in Microsoft Purview apply at the service level: Exchange Online inspects email in transit, SharePoint and OneDrive inspect files at rest and in motion, and Teams inspects messages and shared files. All of that inspection happens server-side. The endpoint workload is different.
Endpoint DLP installs a policy enforcement agent on the Windows device itself. That agent monitors file operations locally and enforces policy before data leaves the machine — not after it reaches a cloud service. This means it can intercept a USB copy operation before the file is ever written to the drive, and it can block a print job before it leaves the print spooler. It operates even when the device is offline. Source: Microsoft Learn — Learn about Endpoint data loss prevention, retrieved 2026-05-23.
Two further differences worth noting. First, file detection uses MIME type inspection, not just file extension. Renaming financial-report.xlsx to picture.jpg does not evade detection — Purview reads the file header and applies SIT matching to the actual content. Second, Endpoint DLP applies at the application layer for print and clipboard operations, which means policy applies whether the user prints from Word, Chrome, or a custom application.
Licensing: What E3 Gives You and What It Does Not Microsoft 365 E3 includes the standard DLP workloads: Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, OneDrive, and Teams. It does not include Endpoint DLP. This is a firm licensing boundary, not a configuration limitation.
Microsoft 365 E5 includes Endpoint DLP as part of the Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance suite. If your organisation is on E3, the Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance add-on makes Endpoint DLP available without upgrading the full E5 license. The add-on is licensed per user and must be assigned to every user whose devices will be subject to Endpoint DLP policies.
Microsoft 365 Business Premium also includes Endpoint DLP for organisations with fewer than 300 users, making it accessible to mid-market customers without a full E5 investment.
License Endpoint DLP included Microsoft 365 E3 No Microsoft 365 E5 Yes Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance add-on (on E3) Yes Microsoft 365 Business Premium Yes (up to 300 users) Source: Microsoft Learn — Microsoft Purview Compliance licensing, retrieved 2026-05-23.
Onboarding Windows Devices to Microsoft Purview Before any Endpoint DLP policy can apply, devices must be onboarded to Microsoft Purview. Onboarding registers the device with the Purview compliance service and deploys the monitoring agent. Supported platforms include Windows 10 (version 20H2 or later), Windows 11, Windows Server 2019, and Windows Server 2022. macOS Catalina 10.15.7 and later is also supported.
Microsoft provides five onboarding methods:
1. Local script — Downloads a script from the compliance portal and runs it manually on each device. Suitable for testing with 10 or fewer devices. Not scalable to production.
2. Group Policy — Deploys the onboarding package via GPO. Suitable for on-premises Active Directory environments without Intune. Requires a package downloaded from the compliance portal.
3. Microsoft Intune / MDM — The preferred method for cloud-managed fleets. Create a device configuration profile of type “Endpoint Detection and Response” using the onboarding package from Purview. This is the same package used for Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, and if MDE is already deployed the devices may already be onboarded.
4. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint shared onboarding — If your tenant has MDE deployed, enabling the Purview integration in Microsoft Defender settings automatically onboards MDE-enrolled devices to Purview without additional configuration. This is the fastest path for organisations already running Defender.
5. Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager (SCCM/ConfigMgr) — Suitable for large hybrid environments. Deploys the onboarding package as a configuration item.
After onboarding, devices appear in the Microsoft Purview compliance portal → Settings → Device onboarding → Devices list. Allow up to 24 hours for a newly onboarded device to become eligible for policy assignment.
Virtualised environments require additional planning. In Azure Virtual Desktop, Windows 365 Cloud PC, and Citrix environments, USB devices are often presented as network redirected drives rather than physical USB devices. Endpoint DLP can still intercept these operations via the “Copy to network share” or “Copy to removable media” activities, but you must verify the activity type matches your policy conditions. Test policy behaviour explicitly in your VDI environment before enforcing block actions.
The 13 Monitored Endpoint Activities Once a device is onboarded, the Endpoint DLP agent monitors the following activities for any file that matches a sensitive information type condition:
Activity Description Copy to USB removable media File write operations to drives classified as removable Copy to network share File write to UNC paths or mapped drives Print Document sent to any printer (local or network) Copy to clipboard Content placed in the system clipboard Upload to cloud services Browser-based upload to cloud storage domains Access by unallowed apps File opened by an application on the restricted apps list Access by unallowed browsers File accessed via a browser not on the allowed list Copy or move via RDP File transfer during a Remote Desktop session Bluetooth transfer File transfer via paired Bluetooth device Copy or move via airdrop (macOS) AirDrop transfer on macOS devices Create item File creation on the device matching SIT conditions Rename item File rename operation on matching content Windows Recall snapshot Content captured by the Windows Recall AI feature Windows Recall monitoring is a 2024 addition. If your policies predate the Windows 11 24H2 release, verify that your policies include this activity if Recall is deployed in your environment. Source: Microsoft Learn — Endpoint activities you can monitor and take action on, retrieved 2026-05-23.
Configuring Your First Endpoint DLP Policy Endpoint DLP policies are created and managed in the Microsoft Purview compliance portal → Data loss prevention → Policies → Create policy. The workflow is the same as for Exchange or SharePoint policies: choose a template or create a custom policy, define the rules, and set the scope.
Scope the policy to devices. On the “Locations” page, enable Devices and disable any workloads you do not want this specific policy to cover. A dedicated endpoint policy is easier to tune than one combined policy that spans Exchange, SharePoint, and Devices.
Set your conditions using sensitive information types (SITs). The condition “Content contains” lets you select from over 300 built-in SITs or custom SITs your organisation has defined. Effective starting conditions for most organisations include:
Credit card numbers EU debit card numbers UK National Insurance numbers (or equivalents for your region) Azure storage connection strings Generic password in file Combine multiple SITs using Any of these or require a minimum instance count to reduce false positives. A rule that triggers on a single credit card number in a file will generate far more alerts than one requiring five or more distinct credit card numbers.
Apply SIT confidence thresholds. Each SIT returns a high, medium, or low confidence match. For block actions on endpoint devices, using only high confidence matches reduces the risk of blocking legitimate business operations. Start with high confidence during the audit phase before expanding to medium confidence once your baseline is established.
Endpoint DLP Actions Each rule in an Endpoint DLP policy allows a different action per endpoint activity. Unlike Exchange DLP — where you typically either allow or block — Endpoint DLP supports three enforcement modes:
Audit only — The activity is permitted but logged to Activity Explorer. No user notification. Use this mode when onboarding new device groups or adding new SIT conditions. Run in audit mode for at least two weeks before moving to restrictive actions.
Block with override — The activity is blocked and the user sees a policy tip. The user can provide a business justification and proceed. The override is logged. This mode balances enforcement with business continuity: users who have a legitimate reason can still complete the operation, and you see the override rate in reporting.
Block — The activity is blocked without an override option. The user sees a policy tip explaining why the action was blocked. Use this mode for your highest-risk activities once audit data confirms low false-positive rates.
Per-activity granularity means you can apply different modes to different activities within the same rule. A common pattern: set USB copy to Block, print to Block with override, and cloud upload to Audit while you build your approved service domains list.
Endpoint-Specific Settings The Endpoint DLP settings page (compliance portal → Data loss prevention → Endpoint DLP settings) controls global device behaviour that applies across all endpoint policies.
USB device groups — Define groups of approved USB devices by vendor ID, product ID, serial number range, or a combination. Use these groups in policy conditions to allow specific approved devices (for example, corporate-issued encrypted drives) while blocking all others. Source: Microsoft Learn — Configure endpoint DLP settings, retrieved 2026-05-23.
Printer groups — Define groups of approved printers by printer name pattern, port, or network share path. Use these in policy conditions to allow printing to corporate print servers while blocking local or Bluetooth printers.
Restricted apps and app groups — List applications that should trigger a block or audit event when they access a sensitive file, regardless of what the application does with the data. This is useful for blocking known data exfiltration tools, personal cloud sync clients (Dropbox, personal OneDrive), or screenshot utilities.
Unallowed browsers — Specify browsers that should be blocked from uploading sensitive files. By default, users can use any browser to upload to cloud services. Configuring unallowed browsers forces users to work within browsers that are covered by the Purview browser extension.
File path exclusions — Exclude specific local paths from policy evaluation. Use this for development directories or backup agents that need unrestricted file access. Be conservative with exclusions — they are a common misconfiguration that creates blind spots.
Sensitive service domains — For the “Upload to cloud services” activity, define which domains are monitored. A corporate SharePoint tenant should typically be excluded; consumer cloud storage services should be monitored.
Policy Tips on Windows Devices Policy tips in Endpoint DLP appear as a dialog or notification in the application where the user is performing the restricted activity — in Explorer for USB/network share operations, in the print dialog for print activities, and in the browser for cloud uploads.
Policy tips can be customised with:
A custom notification message explaining the policy A link to a company policy page or IT support portal Business justification options (free text or a predefined list for block-with-override rules) Effective policy tip messages name the specific SIT detected (“Your document contains credit card numbers”), reference the business policy (“Company policy restricts copying financial data to external devices”), and provide a clear path for exceptions (“Contact IT if you need a temporary exception”). Vague messages (“This operation is not permitted”) lead to help desk tickets rather than informed user decisions.
Monitoring with Activity Explorer and PowerShell Activity Explorer (compliance portal → Data loss prevention → Activity Explorer) is the primary monitoring surface for Endpoint DLP events. Each logged event includes: timestamp, user, device name, activity type, file name and path, SIT detected, confidence level, policy name, rule name, and the action taken (audit, blocked, overridden).
Filter by Activity type = Endpoint activities and Policy = [your policy name] to isolate endpoint DLP events from Exchange and SharePoint DLP noise. Export to CSV for trend analysis or to validate that your block actions are triggering on the expected file types.
To verify your Endpoint DLP policies programmatically, use the Security & Compliance PowerShell module:
#Requires -Modules ExchangeOnlineManagement Connect-IPPSSession -UserPrincipalName admin@contoso.onmicrosoft.com # List all DLP policies scoped to the Endpoint workload $params = @{ ErrorAction = 'Stop' } Get-DlpCompliancePolicy @params | Where-Object { $_.Workload -like '*Endpoint*' } | Select-Object Name, Mode, Priority, Workload | Format-Table -AutoSize # Retrieve rules for a specific policy $policyParams = @{ Policy = 'Endpoint - Block USB Exfiltration' ErrorAction = 'Stop' } Get-DlpComplianceRule @policyParams | Select-Object Name, ContentContainsSensitiveInformation, BlockAccess Connect-IPPSSession authenticates to the Security & Compliance endpoint rather than Exchange Online. If you are already connected via Connect-ExchangeOnline, you still need a separate Connect-IPPSSession call for compliance cmdlets. The ExchangeOnlineManagement module (v3.x or later) provides both connection commands.
Use Get-DlpCompliancePolicy during rollout to confirm that policies are in the expected mode (-Mode TestWithNotifications for audit, Enforce for enforcement) before enabling block actions across your device fleet.
Conclusion Microsoft Purview Endpoint DLP fills the gap that service-level DLP policies leave open: file operations that happen entirely on the device, before any cloud service is involved. The rollout sequence that minimises disruption is: onboard devices → deploy in audit mode → review Activity Explorer for two weeks → tune SIT conditions and exclusions → enable block with override → promote high-confidence, high-risk activities to full block.
The licensing requirement is a hard constraint: E3 tenants need the E5 Compliance add-on before a single Endpoint DLP policy can be enforced. Verify licensing in the compliance portal before beginning device onboarding.
For the policy anatomy that underpins Endpoint DLP rules — how conditions, exceptions, and actions compose — see the Microsoft 365 DLP policy configuration guide. For access controls that complement DLP by restricting which apps and services users can reach, see the Conditional Access best practices guide.
summary: The IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024 puts the average breach cost at $4.88 million — and breaches traced to malicious insiders average nearly double that. DLP policies applied to email and SharePoint miss the most common exfiltration path: a user copying a file to a USB drive, printing it to a personal printer, or uploading it via an unmanaged browser. Microsoft Purview Endpoint DLP closes that gap. This post walks you through onboarding Windows devices, configuring policies with sensitive information type conditions, setting enforcement actions, and monitoring activity in Activity Explorer.
title: Purview Endpoint DLP: Block Data Exfiltration from Windows Devices

content:
Every Microsoft 365 E3 organisation already holds the licence to configure powerful Conditional Access (CA) policies today. Entra ID P1 is included in M365 E3, and it covers named locations, authentication strengths, device compliance grants, and app protection controls. That’s a real Zero Trust foundation, not just an MFA toggle. What E5 adds is automated, continuous risk response: Entra ID Protection feeds real-time sign-in and user risk signals directly into CA policy decisions. This guide walks through the full feature set, from policy anatomy to safe rollout, so you can build the right policies for the licences you hold now and make a clear-eyed decision about upgrading.
Conditional Access is the policy engine at the centre of Microsoft’s Zero Trust architecture. Photo: FlyD via Unsplash.
Key Takeaways
Conditional Access requires Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3 and Business Premium. Risk-based policies (sign-in risk, user risk) require Entra ID P2, available in Microsoft 365 E5 or as an add-on. “Require approved client app” was retired in March 2026. Migrate existing policies to “Require app protection policy.” Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only mode before enforcing any policy (Source: Microsoft Learn, retrieved 2026-05-22). CA App Control requires a separate Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps licence, not included in E3 or standalone P1/P2. What Is the Conditional Access Policy Model? Conditional Access uses a two-phase evaluation model for every sign-in attempt. Phase 1 collects session details: user identity, device state, location, client application, and the target resource. Phase 2 enforces the policies that match those collected signals. Assignments between categories use AND logic, so a user must satisfy all configured conditions for a policy to apply. Within grant controls, the default behaviour is “require all selected controls.” A single tenant can hold up to 240 policies across all states (enabled, disabled, and report-only). Source: Microsoft Learn, “What is Conditional Access”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Grant controls are evaluated in a fixed order when multiple grants are required: MFA, device marked as compliant, hybrid join, approved client app, app protection policy, password change, terms of use, then custom controls. Understanding that order matters when you stack controls. If any Block policy applies to a sign-in, it wins regardless of other grant policies in the chain. Keep your scope precise. A broad Block policy will catch more users than intended.
What Do You Need to Set Up Before Configuring Policies? Two things must be in place before you touch the policy blade: break-glass accounts and report-only mode discipline. Break-glass accounts are cloud-only Global Administrator accounts excluded from every Conditional Access policy without exception. They exist to recover access if a misconfigured policy locks out all admins. Exclude them by account object, not by group, because group membership can change.
Report-only mode is mandatory as the first state for any new policy. It runs full policy evaluation and logs results without enforcing any action. Outcomes appear under Sign-in logs > Report-only and carry one of four states: Success, Failure, User action required, and Not applied. Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only before switching to enforcement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access report-only mode”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Two caveats worth knowing early. The CA Insights workbook (which visualises report-only trends over time) requires Azure Monitor integration. Also, device compliance evaluation in report-only mode on macOS, iOS, and Android can prompt users to pick a certificate. Exclude those platforms from report-only device compliance rules if you don’t want unexpected end-user prompts during testing.
How Do Assignments Define Who, What, and Where a Policy Applies? Assignments are the conditional side of the if-then model. The Users assignment supports inclusions and exclusions across individual accounts, groups, directory roles, and guest or external user types. Always define both sides: who is in scope and who is explicitly excluded. Roles are particularly useful for targeting privileged access policies at Global Administrators, Security Administrators, and Exchange Administrators without building bespoke groups.
The Target resources assignment covers cloud apps, app filter groups, authentication context, and Global Secure Access. Authentication context lets you require step-up authentication for specific sensitive actions inside an app, such as accessing a high-sensitivity SharePoint site, without requiring stronger auth for the entire application.
Conditions refine assignment scope further. The key conditions are:
Device platforms: apply different controls to Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Linux. Client apps: distinguish browser sessions from modern auth clients or legacy authentication protocols. Filter for devices: use device attribute rules (for example, device.extensionAttribute1 -eq "Privileged") to target specific hardware classes. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Assignments”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
How Do Named Locations Strengthen Your Network Conditions? Named locations give Conditional Access a geographic and network context it would otherwise lack. Two location types are available: IP-based and Country-based. A tenant supports up to 195 named locations, each holding up to 2,000 IP ranges in CIDR notation (minimum prefix /8). IP-based locations can be marked as trusted, which improves Entra ID Protection’s risk scoring accuracy. A sign-in from a trusted location is less likely to be flagged as atypical. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Network assignment”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Country-based locations use IP geolocation data by default. Microsoft Authenticator can optionally supply GPS coordinates for the same evaluation, giving higher precision for mobile sign-ins.
VPN users are the most common source of named location confusion. The policy evaluates the VPN exit IP, not the user’s physical location. An employee working in Berlin through a UK exit node will match UK-based location policies. For organisations moving to a Zero Trust network model, Global Secure Access provides source IP restoration: the policy evaluates the user’s actual device IP rather than the VPN egress point.
One UI change to note: the assignment label was renamed from “Location” to “Network” in the Entra admin centre. Existing policies configured against the old label continue to work without changes, but new policy creation uses the updated label.
Which Grant Controls Are Available on E3 Today? Every grant control below is available with Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3. You don’t need E5 to build a strong access control posture. The six primary grant controls cover the full range from hard block to conditional access based on device health and application management state. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Grant controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Block access is the simplest and most absolute control. When a Block policy applies, no other grant policy overrides it.
Require MFA triggers the user’s configured authentication method. Windows Hello for Business satisfies this control for compliant Windows devices.
Require authentication strength is a more precise version of “Require MFA.” Three built-in tiers are available:
Tier Allowed Methods MFA strength Password + Authenticator push, FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA), Temporary Access Pass, OATH tokens Passwordless MFA strength Authenticator phone sign-in, FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA) Phishing-resistant MFA strength FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business / platform credential, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA) Custom authentication strengths are available when built-in tiers don’t match your method mix. One constraint: you cannot combine “Require MFA” and “Require authentication strength” in the same policy. Pick one. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Authentication strengths”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Require device marked as compliant relies on Intune compliance policies. Supported platforms are Windows 10+, iOS, Android, macOS, and Linux Ubuntu. An Intune licence is required separately; it doesn’t come with Entra ID P1 alone.
Require Microsoft Entra hybrid joined device applies only to Windows devices joined to both on-premises Active Directory and Entra ID. iOS, Android, and macOS are excluded from this control.
Require app protection policy enforces Intune Mobile Application Management (MAM). iOS and Android are generally available. Windows is in preview. This control is the correct migration target for any policy that previously used “Require approved client app.”
Deprecation alert: “Require approved client app” was retired in March 2026. Any existing Conditional Access policy that uses this grant control as the only option must be migrated to “Require app protection policy.” If an OR combination of both controls exists in a policy, it continues to work while apps are updated. Three apps do not support app protection policy: Kaizala, Skype for Business, and Visio. For those apps, “Require approved client app” must remain where still applicable. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Grant controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Licence: All grant controls in this section (including authentication strengths) require Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3, Microsoft 365 F1/F3, Microsoft 365 Business Premium, and EMS E3. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What Session Controls Can You Apply with a P1 Licence? Session controls adjust what authenticated users can do after sign-in, without blocking access entirely. Four session controls are available on Entra ID P1. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Session controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Sign-in frequency sets how often a user must reauthenticate. You can require reauthentication on every access attempt (useful for privileged role sessions) or set a configurable interval in hours or days. The default persistent session behaviour in most browsers keeps users signed in far longer than security posture warrants for sensitive applications.
Persistent browser session controls whether the browser stores a persistent session cookie. Setting this to “Never persistent” forces reauthentication when the browser closes, which is appropriate for shared or unmanaged devices.
App-enforced restrictions delegate session control to SharePoint Online and Exchange Online directly. SharePoint uses this to enforce limited-access views, for example blocking downloads from unmanaged devices without needing Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps.
Conditional Access App Control is covered in the next section. It appears as a session control toggle in the CA policy interface, but it requires a separate licence not included with Entra ID P1.
How Does Conditional Access App Control Work, and What Does It Cost? Conditional Access App Control uses a reverse proxy architecture to sit between the user’s browser and the target application. Non-Edge browsers access the app through a URL with the *.mcas.ms suffix appended. Microsoft Edge gets native in-browser protection without the URL modification. The proxy intercepts session activity and can apply granular session policies regardless of whether the app is managed by your organisation. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
The feature works with SAML 2.0 and OIDC apps, including third-party SaaS applications onboarded to Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps (MDCA). It also works with on-premises apps published through the Entra application proxy.
Session policy activities available through CAAC:
Prevent data exfiltration: block download, cut, copy, and print within the session. Protect on download: apply Microsoft Purview sensitivity labels to downloaded files instead of blocking them entirely. If you haven’t yet configured those labels, see the guide on how to configure DLP policies in Microsoft 365. Prevent upload of unlabeled files: stop users from uploading unclassified content to sanctioned apps. Block potential malware uploads: scan uploads for known malware signatures before they reach the app. Monitor sessions: log all session activity for compliance and investigation without enforcing any block. Two limitations catch organisations by surprise. First, the Teams desktop application is NOT supported for CAAC. Browser sessions only. If your session policy targets Teams, unmanaged users on the desktop client fall outside the proxy entirely. Second, session policies have a 50 MB file size limit. Files above that threshold bypass session inspection entirely. Design download controls with this limit in mind when handling large engineering or finance files.
Licence: Conditional Access App Control requires Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps. This is included in Microsoft 365 E5 Security, Microsoft 365 E5, and EMS E5. It is NOT included in Microsoft 365 E3 or standalone Entra ID P1/P2 licences. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What Does Upgrading to E5 Actually Unlock? Risk-Based Conditional Access Risk-based Conditional Access is the primary identity security upgrade that E5 unlocks. Entra ID Protection runs a continuous machine-learning analysis of every sign-in and user account in your tenant. It surfaces two risk signals: sign-in risk (the probability that a specific authentication attempt is not from the legitimate user) and user risk (the probability that a user account has been compromised). Both require Entra ID P2. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Risk-based Conditional Access policies”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Sign-In Risk Policy Microsoft recommends targeting medium AND high sign-in risk levels. Low risk generates too much noise for a production enforcement policy. The recommended grant configuration pairs authentication strength (built-in MFA strength) with a session control of sign-in frequency: every time, forcing full reauthentication on each risky sign-in.
For passwordless deployments, use Passwordless MFA strength or Phishing-resistant MFA strength instead, so users who have already removed passwords aren’t prompted to use a method they don’t have.
One critical prerequisite: users who have not completed MFA registration and trigger a risky sign-in receive error AADSTS53004 and are completely blocked. They cannot self-remediate. Ensure organisation-wide MFA registration is complete before enabling sign-in risk enforcement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access policy: Sign-in risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
User Risk Policy Microsoft recommends targeting high user risk only. Medium user risk generates too many false positives for automated enforcement. The grant for user risk is “Require risk remediation”, which automatically applies authentication strength and sets sign-in frequency to every time.
User risk enables Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) self-remediation. The user resets their password, Entra ID Protection reassesses the risk level, and the account is unblocked without helpdesk involvement. This is the operational efficiency argument for P2 beyond the security posture improvement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access policy: User risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Exclude service accounts and break-glass accounts from both risk policies. Service accounts typically exhibit atypical sign-in patterns by design, and risk policies will generate constant alerts for them.
The real upgrade value is continuous, automated response. Without P2, your process for responding to a compromised credential is: detection, alert, analyst review, ticket opened, helpdesk calls the user. With P2, the policy triggers MFA remediation or SSPR the moment the risk fires. The attack window shrinks from hours to seconds.
Licence: Sign-in risk and user risk conditions in Conditional Access require Entra ID P2, included in Microsoft 365 E5, Microsoft Defender Suite, and EMS E5. Entra ID P2 is also available as an add-on for Microsoft 365 E3. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
How Do You Roll Out Conditional Access Policies Safely? A phased rollout is not optional. CA policies are additive, and any applicable Block policy wins regardless of the order policies are evaluated. Rushing to enforcement before validating report-only results is the most common cause of lockout incidents. The three-phase model below applies to any new policy, not just a full deployment. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Plan a Conditional Access deployment”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Phase Timeline Policies Minimum Report-Only Period Phase 1 Weeks 1-2 Block legacy authentication, secure MFA registration, phishing-resistant MFA for privileged roles 1 week Phase 2 Weeks 2-3 Strong authentication for all users and guests, app protection policy for mobile, require MFA for device join/registration 1 week Phase 3 Weeks 3-4 Sign-in risk policy, user risk policy, token protection, device code flow restriction (P2 required) 1 week Phase 1 is the highest-value starting point. Legacy authentication protocols (Basic Auth, IMAP, POP3, SMTP Auth) bypass modern CA entirely. Securing the MFA registration page closes the registration loophole: without it, an attacker with a stolen password can register their own MFA device before the legitimate user notices. Use FIDO2 or Windows Hello for Business for privileged roles, not Authenticator push.
Phase 2 extends strong authentication to the broader user population. App protection policy for iOS and Android closes the unmanaged mobile access gap without requiring device enrolment in Intune.
Phase 3 requires Entra ID P2. Token protection and device code flow restriction reduce token theft and phishing-resistant bypass techniques. Every phase starts in report-only. No exceptions.
Licence Comparison: E3, E5, and the P2 Add-On The table below maps each Conditional Access feature to its minimum licence requirement. Use it as a scoping reference before your rollout kickoff call. Features that appear gated may simply require an add-on you already have but haven’t assigned. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Feature E3 (Entra P1) Entra P2 add-on on E3 E5 (Entra P2) MDCA (separate) Standard CA policies Yes Yes Yes N/A Named locations Yes Yes Yes N/A Authentication strengths Yes Yes Yes N/A Report-only mode Yes Yes Yes N/A Device compliance grant (Intune) Yes* Yes* Yes* N/A App protection policy grant Yes Yes Yes N/A Sign-in risk condition No Yes Yes N/A User risk condition No Yes Yes N/A Entra ID Protection reports No Yes Yes N/A CA App Control (MCAS) No No No** Yes *Intune licence required separately for device compliance.
**Included in M365 E5 Security / E5 which bundles MDCA; standalone E5 without the Security add-on may require MDCA separately. Verify your current bundle against the Microsoft product terms.
The Entra P2 add-on for E3 is the most cost-efficient path to risk-based CA without a full E5 migration. It delivers sign-in risk, user risk, and Entra ID Protection reporting for identity security teams that don’t yet need the full E5 workload bundle.
Frequently Asked Questions What is the difference between report-only mode and disabled in Conditional Access? A disabled policy is completely inactive and logs nothing. Report-only mode runs full policy evaluation and logs the outcome for every matching sign-in, but does not enforce any grant or session control. The four logged outcome states are: Success, Failure, User action required, and Not applied. Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only before enforcement for any new policy. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Report-only mode”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Can Microsoft 365 E3 users configure risk-based Conditional Access? No. Sign-in risk and user risk conditions require Entra ID P2. E3 includes P1 only. E3 organisations can purchase the Entra P2 add-on per user to unlock risk-based CA without upgrading to E5. Alternatively, a full upgrade to Microsoft 365 E5 includes P2 across the board. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What should I do with existing “Require approved client app” policies? Migrate them to “Require app protection policy” before the retirement breaks them. The safest path: add “Require app protection policy” as an OR option alongside the existing grant, test in report-only, then remove the deprecated grant once validation is complete. Three exceptions remain: Kaizala, Skype for Business, and Visio must continue using “Require approved client app” where still applicable.
Why are users getting AADSTS53004 errors after enabling sign-in risk policies? AADSTS53004 means the user has not completed MFA registration but triggered a risky sign-in. Risk policies require MFA remediation, which is impossible without a registered MFA method. Complete MFA registration for all users before enabling sign-in risk enforcement. Run the Authentication Methods Activity report in the Entra admin centre to identify unregistered users first. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Sign-in risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Does Conditional Access App Control work with the Microsoft Teams desktop app? No. The Teams desktop application is not supported for CA App Control. CAAC applies only to browser sessions. Users on the Teams desktop client bypass the proxy entirely. Users accessing Teams through a browser on an unmanaged device are subject to CAAC session policies. Design your unmanaged device policies with this boundary in mind. Source: Microsoft Learn, “CA App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Start in Report-Only Mode, Then Build From There Conditional Access is not a deploy-once configuration. It grows with your organisation’s licence posture, device management maturity, and risk tolerance. Start by reviewing any existing “Require approved client app” policies, since those need migration now. Audit your break-glass accounts and verify they’re excluded from every policy. Then run your first new policy in report-only for at least a week before enforcing.
For E5 evaluation: activate an Entra ID P2 trial and enable the sign-in risk policy in report-only mode for two weeks. The Entra ID Protection risky sign-ins report will show you real risk events from your tenant. That live data is far more persuasive for an upgrade business case than any vendor comparison sheet.
The Zero Trust identity layer starts with P1 and the policies you can build today. P2 adds the automated risk response layer on top. Both are worth building deliberately rather than fast. For the broader data-protection picture, see the guide on how to configure DLP policies in Microsoft 365 — Conditional Access and DLP work as complementary layers in a defence-in-depth strategy. Browse all posts in the Security and Microsoft 365 categories for related coverage on identity and access management.
summary: Every Microsoft 365 E3 organisation already holds the licence to configure powerful Conditional Access (CA) policies today. Entra ID P1 is included in M365 E3, and it covers named locations, authentication strengths, device compliance grants, and app protection controls. That’s a real Zero Trust foundation, not just an MFA toggle. What E5 adds is automated, continuous risk response: Entra ID Protection feeds real-time sign-in and user risk signals directly into CA policy decisions. This guide walks through the full feature set, from policy anatomy to safe rollout, so you can build the right policies for the licences you hold now and make a clear-eyed decision about upgrading.
title: Complete Guide: Conditional Access Policies in Entra ID

content:
In the first half of 2025, identity-based attacks against Microsoft 365 tenants surged 32%, according to the Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2025. Microsoft now processes over 600 million identity attacks every single day. Conditional Access is the policy engine designed to stop them — but only if it’s configured correctly.
Most organisations have Conditional Access available and haven’t deployed it. Others deployed it years ago and haven’t reviewed it since. Both are dangerous. This guide doesn’t explain what Conditional Access is. For that, see our complete guide to Conditional Access policies in Entra ID. This guide tells you what to actually build: which policies, in which order, with which settings, and how to automate the deployment with Microsoft Graph PowerShell.
Conditional Access is only as effective as the policies you deploy. Photo: jonakoh / Unsplash.
Key Takeaways
In 2025, 99.9% of compromised M365 accounts had no MFA (Microsoft Partner Center, 2025) — CA policies are the fix. M365 E3 (Entra ID P1) covers the full CA policy engine; E5 (Entra ID P2) adds risk-based automated response. A 2026 enforcement change applies “All resources” CA policies to OIDC flows regardless of exclusions — audit your tenant now. Why Can’t Conditional Access Wait in 2025? In 2025, more than 99.9% of compromised Microsoft 365 accounts had no MFA enabled, according to Microsoft Learn’s Partner Center security statistics, retrieved 2026-05-22. That figure has been consistent for years. The attack methods evolve — password spray, phishing, adversary-in-the-middle token theft — but the underlying gap stays the same. Unprotected accounts are the path of least resistance, and attackers know it.
There’s also a 2026 action item most guides don’t mention. Microsoft is rolling out an enforcement change between March and June 2026: Conditional Access policies targeting “All resources” will now be enforced for OIDC-scope sign-ins even when resource exclusions are present. If your tenant has a policy that uses “All resources” with specific app exclusions, users in affected sign-in flows will start receiving unexpected MFA or device compliance prompts. Check those policies now before the rollout hits your tenant. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Common security policies for Microsoft 365”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
And if you have “Require approved client app” in any policy, it’s already retired as of March 2026. The replacement is “Require app protection policy,” which provides the same data loss protection with broader device coverage — including unenrolled devices.
According to the Microsoft-managed Conditional Access policies documentation, retrieved 2026-05-22, MFA continues to reduce the risk of account compromise by more than 99%. That’s not a number you ignore. The question isn’t whether to deploy Conditional Access. It’s how to do it without locking out your users in the first week.
What Does Microsoft 365 E3 Give You for Conditional Access? In 2025, a frequent misconception among IT teams is that Conditional Access is an “E5 feature.” It isn’t. Microsoft 365 E3 includes Entra ID P1, which covers the complete CA policy engine: named locations, authentication strength, device compliance grants, app protection controls, and the ability to block legacy authentication outright. Source: Microsoft Learn, Entra ID licensing, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What E5 adds is automated risk response. Entra ID P2 — included in Microsoft 365 E5 and the Microsoft Defender Suite — enables risk-based Conditional Access: policies that evaluate real-time sign-in risk and user risk signals from Entra ID Protection and respond automatically. You can’t replicate that with E3 policies and manual reviews. It’s a fundamentally different capability.
Conditional Access: M365 E3 (Entra ID P1) vs M365 E5 (Entra ID P2) Conditional Access: E3 (P1) vs E5 (P2) Feature M365 E3 (Entra P1) M365 E5 (Entra P2) CA Policy Engine (MFA, conditions, grants)✓✓ Block Legacy Authentication✓✓ Named Locations & Geo-blocking✓✓ Device Compliance (requires Intune)✓✓ Authentication Strength (FIDO2 / WHfB)✓✓ App Protection Policy (Intune MAM)✓✓ — Entra ID P2 required below this line — Sign-in Risk Policies (ID Protection)✗✓ User Risk Policies & Privileged Identity Management✗✓ Source: Microsoft Learn — Entra ID Licensing, retrieved 2026-05-22 E3 (Entra ID P1) covers the full CA policy engine. E5 (Entra ID P2) adds automated risk-based response — and that gap can't be bridged manually. The practical decision is this: don’t wait for an E5 upgrade to start deploying CA. E3 blocks the majority of real-world attacks. E5 adds the automated, continuous response layer for high-value targets and regulated environments. Build your P1 foundation first, then layer P2 on top if you have the licence.
According to Microsoft Learn’s Entra ID licensing documentation, retrieved 2026-05-22, Microsoft Entra ID P1 is included in Microsoft 365 E3, F1, F3, and Business Premium. Entra ID P2 — which adds risk-based Conditional Access via ID Protection — is included in Microsoft 365 E5 and the Microsoft Defender Suite. The capability difference isn’t marginal: risk-based policies respond to signals that no manual policy can replicate.
How Should You Phase a Conditional Access Rollout? Deploying CA policies all at once is how you end up rolling everything back on day two. Microsoft’s own deployment guide — Plan a Conditional Access deployment, retrieved 2026-05-22 — recommends a phased approach across four weeks. Each phase builds on the last. You don’t move to Phase 2 until Phase 1 has run in report-only mode for at least a week and you’ve verified the sign-in logs.
Before you touch Phase 1, two prerequisites must be in place. First: create two or more break-glass accounts — cloud-only Global Administrator accounts excluded from every CA policy by object ID, not by group. These are your recovery path if a misconfigured policy locks everyone out. Second: confirm every user you’re targeting has registered an MFA method. A policy that enforces MFA against users who haven’t registered yet blocks them completely.
Conditional Access Phased Deployment: 4-Week Rollout Conditional Access: 4-Week Phased Deployment Week 1 Week 2 Week 3 Week 4 Phase 1 Foundation P1 only Phase 2 Core Auth P1 only Phase 3 Advanced P2 req. Block legacy auth Secure MFA registration Admin phishing-resistant MFA MFA for all users + guests App protection on mobile MFA for device registration Risk-based policies (sign-in + user) Token protection · Device code block Source: Microsoft Learn — Plan a Conditional Access deployment, retrieved 2026-05-22 Phase 3 (dashed) requires Entra ID P2. Skip it if you're on E3 — Phases 1 and 2 alone block the vast majority of identity attacks. Phase 1 — Foundation (Week 1–2): Three policies, all Entra ID P1. Block legacy authentication across all users and all apps. Secure the MFA registration page so users can only register from a trusted location (preventing attackers from registering their own MFA methods on a compromised account). Require phishing-resistant MFA — specifically FIDO2 security keys or Windows Hello for Business via authentication strength — for privileged directory roles.
Phase 2 — Core Authentication (Week 2–3): Four policies, all Entra ID P1. Require MFA for all users targeting all resources with no app exclusions. Extend MFA to guest accounts. Require app protection policy for iOS and Android. Require MFA for device join and device registration. By the end of Phase 2, every interactive sign-in from every user is covered.
Phase 3 — Advanced Protection (Week 3–4): This phase requires Entra ID P2. Block high-risk sign-ins. Require MFA or password change for high-risk users. Add token protection, device code flow restriction, and authentication transfer blocking. If you’re on E3, document this phase as a known capability gap and revisit when you add E5 or the E5 Security add-on.
Which Conditional Access Policies Does Every Tenant Need? Every Microsoft 365 tenant with Entra ID P1 should have these six policies deployed and enforced. This isn’t a “nice to have” list — it’s the minimum posture. The CyberCX 2025 analysis by Chris Chambers and Alan Johnstone, retrieved 2026-05-22, found that most tenants they assessed had fewer than four of these in place, with stale exclusions undermining the ones that were deployed.
CA01 — Block Legacy Authentication. Scope: all users, all apps, client app types exchangeActiveSync and other. Grant: Block. Always start here. Legacy authentication protocols can’t satisfy MFA challenges — attackers use them specifically to bypass CA. This policy alone closes one of the widest attack surfaces in any M365 environment.
CA02 — Require MFA for All Users. Scope: all users, all resources. Grant: require authentication strength (multifactor). No app exclusions. Microsoft explicitly recommends against app exclusions in a baseline MFA policy because apps like Teams and Exchange depend on multiple backend services — exclusions create unpredictable behaviour. Exclude your break-glass accounts by object ID.
CA03 — Phishing-Resistant MFA for Privileged Roles. Scope: Global Admins, Security Admins, Exchange Admins (and others with high-privilege roles), all resources. Grant: require authentication strength set to “Phishing-resistant” — FIDO2 security keys or Windows Hello for Business only. Push notifications and SMS don’t qualify. Admin accounts are the highest-value phishing targets.
CA04 — Secure MFA Registration. Scope: all users, user action “Register security information.” Conditions: require network location outside named trusted locations. Grant: require MFA. This prevents an attacker who has captured a password from registering their own MFA device while no MFA exists yet.
CA05 — Require App Protection Policy on Mobile. Scope: all users, Exchange Online and SharePoint, platforms iOS and Android. Grant: require app protection policy. This replaces the retired “Require approved client app.” Intune MAM is required but works on both enrolled and unenrolled devices.
CA06 — Block High-Risk Sign-ins (E5/P2 only). Scope: all users, all resources, sign-in risk: high. Grant: Block. If you’re on E3, document this slot in your policy register as a known gap. The risk-based signals from ID Protection are the automated layer that catches credential stuffing, impossible travel, and adversary-in-the-middle attacks in real time.
One limit worth keeping in mind: tenants have a maximum of 240 CA policies across all states (enabled, disabled, and report-only). Report-only policies count. Don’t accumulate stale test policies — clean them up after each validation cycle. Source: Microsoft Learn, Plan a Conditional Access deployment, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Automating Policy Creation with Microsoft Graph PowerShell Hand-building six (or sixteen) CA policies through the Entra admin centre invites human error and makes rollback painful. Microsoft Graph PowerShell — specifically New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy from the Microsoft.Graph.Identity.SignIns module — is the correct tool for repeatable, auditable policy deployment. Source: Microsoft Learn, New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy, retrieved 2026-05-22.
The required permission scopes are Policy.Read.All and Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess. You’ll need at least the Conditional Access Administrator role.
The example below creates CA01 — Block Legacy Authentication in report-only mode. Change state to "enabled" only after you’ve reviewed a week of sign-in logs and confirmed no legitimate services use legacy auth.
Import-Module Microsoft.Graph.Identity.SignIns Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Policy.Read.All", "Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess" # Replace with your break-glass account object IDs $breakGlassIds = @("<break-glass-account-1-object-id>", "<break-glass-account-2-object-id>") $params = @{ displayName = "CA01 - Block Legacy Authentication" state = "enabledForReportingButNotEnforced" # switch to "enabled" after report-only validation conditions = @{ clientAppTypes = @("exchangeActiveSync", "other") # targets legacy auth clients applications = @{ includeApplications = @("All") } users = @{ includeUsers = @("All") excludeUsers = $breakGlassIds } } grantControls = @{ operator = "OR" builtInControls = @("block") } } New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter $params Write-Host "CA01 created in report-only mode. Review sign-in logs for 7 days before enforcing." exchangeActiveSync covers Exchange ActiveSync clients. other covers older SMTP, IMAP, POP3, and basic auth clients. Together, these two client app types define the full legacy auth surface. Omitting either leaves a gap.
Automating at scale. If you manage multiple tenants or want to deploy all six policies as a baseline, structure each policy as a hashtable in an array and loop with New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy. Store your break-glass object IDs and group IDs in a configuration object at the top, not hardcoded throughout the script.
What If API. As of July 2025, Microsoft released a Conditional Access “What If” API that allows programmatic impact testing before rollout — you pass a simulated sign-in payload and get back a policy evaluation result. This is the equivalent of the portal’s What If tool, callable from your CI/CD pipeline. Source: Microsoft Learn, CA Optimization Agent, retrieved 2026-05-22.
CA Optimization Agent. Released at Microsoft Ignite in November 2025, the Conditional Access Optimization Agent continuously monitors your tenant’s CA policies, flags unprotected users and apps, and surfaces suggested changes. After November 17, 2025, it runs under a dedicated Entra Agent ID rather than the activating user’s identity. It doesn’t replace manual policy design, but it closes the monitoring gap that most teams ignore. For setup instructions, see CA Optimization Agent in the Microsoft Entra documentation.
Five Mistakes That Leave Gaps in Your Coverage The difference between a secure CA deployment and a false sense of security often comes down to five configuration patterns. Chris Chambers and Alan Johnstone from CyberCX documented these from real engagements in their 2025 Conditional Access analysis, retrieved 2026-05-22.
1. Exclusion drift. Every CA policy accumulates exclusions over time — service accounts, VIP users, testing groups. Most of those exclusions were meant to be temporary. Attackers specifically target excluded accounts because they know those accounts bypass CA entirely. Review your exclusion lists quarterly. Any account excluded from your MFA policy needs a documented and active reason.
2. Browser sessions missed. It’s common to see device compliance or app protection controls applied to mobile and desktop clients but not to browser sessions of the same application. A user can open Exchange Online in Chrome on a personal, unmanaged laptop and bypass the controls entirely if the browser session isn’t in scope. Always check client app type coverage in your conditions.
3. Device code flow unblocked. Device code flow — the authentication method used by devices without a browser — is rarely needed in most tenants. It’s also one of the primary vectors for token theft attacks. Block it in Phase 3 (Entra ID P1 required, despite being in the advanced phase). The only users who need it are those using Azure CLI or similar tooling from headless devices, and that can be scoped with a narrow allow-policy.
4. Risk signals ignored. Many E5 tenants have Entra ID Protection active but haven’t wired the sign-in risk and user risk signals into their CA policies. The risk signals fire — the logs show them — but nothing enforces a response. If you have P2 licences, connect the signals. A high-risk sign-in that generates a detection and then proceeds to full access isn’t a security posture improvement.
5. No monitoring cadence. Conditional Access is not set-and-forget. The Entra ID Insights and Reporting workbook tracks the percentage of sign-ins in scope for CA policy, the percentage protected by MFA, and policy-level success and failure rates. Without Azure Monitor integration, this data stays in the sign-in logs but doesn’t aggregate. Build a monthly review of these metrics into your security operations cadence.
Frequently Asked Questions Does Conditional Access work with Microsoft 365 E3? Yes. Microsoft 365 E3 includes Entra ID P1, which gives you the full CA policy engine: named locations, authentication strength (including phishing-resistant MFA), device compliance, app protection policy, and legacy auth blocking. What E3 doesn’t include is risk-based CA — sign-in risk and user risk policies require Entra ID P2 (included in M365 E5). Build your E3 foundation first, then add E5 for automated risk response.
How many Conditional Access policies can a tenant have? The tenant-wide limit is 240 policies across all states — enabled, disabled, and report-only. Report-only policies count against this limit. Stale test policies and abandoned report-only configurations accumulate quickly in active tenants. Set a quarterly cleanup task. If you hit the limit, consolidate per-app policies into grouped resource policies and use filter-for-applications instead of manual app lists. Source: Microsoft Learn, Plan a Conditional Access deployment, 2025.
What replaced “Require approved client app” in Conditional Access? “Require application protection policy” (Intune MAM) is the replacement, effective March 2026. It provides the same data loss protection — and more. Unlike “Require approved client app,” it works on both enrolled and unenrolled devices. The only exception: Kaizala, Skype for Business, and Visio don’t support app protection policy and require the legacy control where still applicable. Migrate any policies using only “Require approved client app” before the control stops functioning.
What is the 2026 “All resources” enforcement change? Between March and June 2026, Microsoft is rolling out a change that enforces CA policies targeting “All resources” against OIDC-scope sign-ins, even when the policy contains resource exclusions. If your tenant has a catch-all MFA policy with specific app exclusions, users accessing excluded apps via OIDC flows may start receiving unexpected MFA challenges. Audit all “All resources” policies with exclusions in the Entra admin centre now. Source: Microsoft Learn, Common security policies for Microsoft 365, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What to Do This Week Conditional Access isn’t complex. It’s just consequential — a mistake in a broad policy affects everyone in your tenant at once. That’s why the phased approach and report-only validation aren’t optional steps you can skip when you’re in a hurry.
Start with Phase 1. Block legacy authentication. Secure the MFA registration page. Require phishing-resistant MFA for your privileged admins. Those three policies, run in report-only for a week and then enforced, close more attack surface than most security projects that take six months.
For the automation path, use New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy and build your policies as code from day one. When your CA baseline is in a script, rolling it out to a new tenant takes minutes, reviewing changes takes a code review, and recovering from a misconfiguration takes a single parameter change.
If you haven’t read our complete guide to Conditional Access policy anatomy and E3/E5 feature details, that’s the reference for when you need to understand why a specific grant control behaves the way it does. This guide is for building what you know you need. That one is for understanding what you’re building.
And if you’re configuring access controls for sensitive data — not just identity — see our guide on configuring DLP policies in Microsoft 365 for the Purview layer that works alongside Conditional Access.
All statistics sourced from Tier 1 Microsoft documentation unless otherwise noted. Sources retrieved 2026-05-22.
Source URL Retrieved Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2025 https://cdn-dynmedia-1.microsoft.com/is/content/microsoftcorp/microsoft/msc/documents/presentations/CSR/Microsoft-Digital-Defense-Report-2025.pdf 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — Partner Center MFA statistics https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/partner-center/security/security-at-your-organization 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — Plan a Conditional Access deployment https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity/conditional-access/plan-conditional-access 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — Microsoft-managed CA policies https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity/conditional-access/managed-policies 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — Entra ID licensing https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/licensing 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — Common security policies for M365 https://learn.microsoft.com/security/zero-trust/zero-trust-identity-device-access-policies-common 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.graph.identity.signins/new-mgidentityconditionalaccesspolicy 2026-05-22 Microsoft Learn — CA Optimization Agent https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/security-copilot/conditional-access-agent-optimization 2026-05-22 CyberCX — Revisiting Conditional Access in 2025 https://cybercx.com.au/blog/revisiting-conditional-access-in-2025 2026-05-22
